detail.falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: NVIDIA's backlog and purchase commitments have grown faster than its shipment volumes, which is the signature of demand pulling ahead of supply rather than supply chasing weak demand.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex guidance and any broad AI-infrastructure spending slowdown that would let shipments catch up to backlog.
detail.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
detail.title: NVDA demand durability

detail.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM lead times normalize to historical norms while demand indicators stay positive.
detail.mechanism: CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity and HBM memory supply, not wafer starts or end demand, set the ceiling on how many finished GPUs can actually ship in a given quarter.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to announced advanced-packaging and HBM capacity additions from the packaging and memory vendors, which would loosen the binding constraint independent of demand.
detail.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
detail.title: Supply constraint binding

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters without a named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale.
detail.mechanism: AMD's Instinct MI-series roadmap and its hyperscaler design-win cadence determine whether it becomes a credible, at-scale second source in datacenter accelerators rather than a niche alternative.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to named hyperscaler deployment announcements and AMD's execution against its MI-series shipment cadence.
detail.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
detail.title: AMD credible second source

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler capex mix shifts back toward merchant GPUs, or a named ASIC program is cancelled/delayed a generation.
detail.mechanism: Hyperscalers' in-house training and inference accelerator programs compete directly with merchant-GPU purchases for the same capex dollars, so their scale-up can substitute for merchant-GPU demand growth.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex-mix disclosures and the shipment/deployment cadence of named custom-silicon programs.
detail.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
detail.title: Custom ASIC substitution

detail.falsifiableTrigger: The D6 price track shows ≥15% decline across ≥2 providers within a quarter.
detail.mechanism: Sustained demand for GPU compute keeps rental and spot prices firm even as new capacity comes online, which indicates the market is not yet oversupplied.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to the D6 rental price track across providers and to the pace at which new GPU capacity enters the rental market.
detail.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
detail.title: Pricing power persistence

detail.falsifiableTrigger: A full licensing regime rollback, or two quarters with no new policy action and no vendor-reported impact.
detail.mechanism: Export-control restrictions on advanced GPUs limit the addressable market in China and other restricted destinations, redirecting or destroying demand that would otherwise be served.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to new export-control rulemaking, licensing decisions, and vendor-reported revenue-impact disclosures tied to restricted destinations.
detail.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
detail.title: Export control exposure

falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Booked orders, financed deployments and multi-GW campus commitments accumulate faster than NVIDIA can ship, sustaining a demand overhang.
rationale: Multiple large fresh design wins and order signals show demand outrunning supply: Anthropic Claude on GB300 in Azure Foundry, Sharon AI deploying up to 40,000 GB300, Firmus 170,000-GPU Indonesia campus, plus NVIDIA's $485B GDP claim and vendors reporting an AI order boom spilling into 2H26 with Vera Rubin demand staying strong through the transition.
sensitivity: Hyperscaler and AI-cloud capex plans and the pace of Vera Rubin supply ramp.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM leadTimes normalize to historical norms for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
mechanism: Advanced-packaging (CoWoS) and HBM capacity gate how much wafer and demand can be converted into shippable accelerators, so realizable revenue tracks packaging/HBM output.
rationale: Supply-side signals stay positive but do not isolate or relieve the packaging/HBM bottleneck: the $500B U.S. buildout explicitly includes packaging partner Amkor, and the Vera Rubin transition finishes 2Q26 with an upswing from 3Q26, implying capacity is still being added rather than lead times normalizing against strong demand.
sensitivity: Packaging (Amkor/TSMC) and HBM capacity additions relative to the Vera Rubin ramp.

falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters without a named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale.
mechanism: AMD ramping Instinct/Helios to mass production creates a viable second source that hyperscalers can adopt to reduce NVIDIA dependence.
rationale: AMD Helios is cited as moving into mass production alongside NVIDIA Vera Rubin, AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU, supporting roadmap execution; however no named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale appears in the findings, so the case for converting the roadmap into material share advances only modestly.
sensitivity: Named hyperscaler adoption commitments and Helios production yield versus NVIDIA availability.

falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler capex mix shifts back toward merchant GPUs for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named ASIC program is cancelled or delayed by 12+ months.
mechanism: Hyperscalers and large AI labs build custom accelerators for better performance-per-watt and NVIDIA independence, diverting incremental workloads off merchant GPUs.
rationale: Custom-silicon momentum broadens: AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU are moving into mass production, OpenAI's Broadcom-built 'Jalapeno' inference chip is cited for better perf-per-watt, and Anthropic is in early talks with Samsung on a 2nm custom chip as part of diversification away from merchant GPUs — though Anthropic simultaneously going GA on GB300 shows displacement of demand growth, not absolute demand.
sensitivity: Custom-silicon perf-per-watt gains versus NVIDIA and whether labs run ASIC and merchant GPUs in parallel rather than substituting.

falsifiableTrigger: The D6 price track shows ≥15% decline across ≥2 providers within a quarter.
mechanism: Persistent supply-demand tightness lets GPU-cloud providers hold on-demand rental rates even as new capacity comes online.
rationale: Rental levels hold: Lambda B200 at $6.69/GPU-hr is unchanged from the prior D6 baseline and H100 SXM sits at $3.99, while CoreWeave lists firm per-instance prices (e.g., HGX B200 $68.80, HGX H100 $49.24); no provider shows the ≥15% decline the trigger requires despite ongoing supply additions.
sensitivity: Pace of new rental capacity coming online versus incremental rental demand.

falsifiableTrigger: A full licensing regime rollback, or two quarters with no new policy action and no vendor-reported impact.
mechanism: Export licensing restricts NVIDIA sales into controlled regions, shrinking addressable merchant-GPU demand when policy tightens.
rationale: No export-control policy action or vendor-reported impact appears this cycle, and international buildouts proceed unhindered — notably Firmus's 360 MW, up-to-170,000-GPU campus in Batam, Indonesia — signaling addressable merchant-GPU demand is being served abroad; this is one clean cycle, short of the two-quarter threshold to break the thesis.
sensitivity: U.S. licensing policy changes and whether controlled-region demand can be redirected to permitted geographies.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA discloses financing receivables rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a financed AI-cloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed order to cash revenue within a quarter.
detail.mechanism: Vendor financing lowers customers' capital barriers and pulls demand forward, but ties NVIDIA revenue recognition to its own credit extension and the solvency of financed AI-cloud buyers.
detail.sensitivity: Credit conditions and the underlying cash solvency of financed AI-cloud customers.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
detail.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA rpoBacklog (multi-year cloud commitments) and its $119B supply commitments grow <10% while Data Center revenue keeps rising, for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Hyperscaler AI capex ($660-690B, heading toward $3-4T) exceeds NVIDIA's packaging/HBM-limited output, so backlog, supply commitments and >12-month lead times accumulate faster than shipments.
rationale: NVIDIA Data Center revenue hit a record $75.2B (+92% YoY) with Q2 guided to $91B assuming zero China, multi-year cloud commitments rose to $30B from $27B, supply commitments reached $119B, and management expects to stay supply-constrained through the entire Rubin generation — demand is outrunning shippable supply more, not less.
sensitivity: Hyperscaler capex trajectory and how much custom-ASIC in-housing diverts incremental demand.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS-S/L leadTimes fall below 26 weeks AND HBM3E returns to available (not sold-out) supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
mechanism: Advanced 2.5D packaging (CoWoS) and HBM stacks are the physical chokepoints for accelerator assembly; with both fully booked, shippable units are capped regardless of wafer starts or order demand.
rationale: CoWoS-S/L is fully booked at 52-78 week lead times, HBM3E is effectively sold out for 2026 with double-digit price gains, TSMC 3nm runs at 100% utilization with demand ~3x supply, and even AMD/Intel cite tight supply — the packaging/HBM bottleneck is more acute and specific than last cycle.
sensitivity: Pace of CoWoS capacity expansion and incremental HBM3E/12-hi supply coming online.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue growth falls below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler MI-series deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct accelerator share stalls below 6%.
mechanism: Hyperscalers seeking supply diversification and lower cost adopt Instinct (Meta 6GW), converting AMD's roadmap into recurring datacenter revenue and share.
rationale: AMD Data Center revenue hit a record $5.8B (+57% YoY) with Q2 guided to $11.2B (+46%), and the Meta 6GW Instinct commitment including a custom MI450 shipping 2H 2026 is a named at-scale hyperscaler win; Instinct is estimated to reach $15B+ and 6-8% accelerator share in 2026 as NVIDIA's share slips toward 75%.
sensitivity: On-schedule MI450 ramp in 2H 2026 and AMD's HBM/CoWoS allocation.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA hyperscale/Data Center revenue growth reaccelerates with no new scaled custom-ASIC deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named ASIC program (Ironwood, Trainium 3, MI450-custom) is cancelled or slips ≥12 months.
mechanism: Hyperscalers with concentrated inference/training workloads build cost-optimized ASICs (Ironwood, Trainium), capturing incremental AI compute that would otherwise be merchant GPUs.
rationale: Substitution is now concrete and large — Anthropic deploys >1M Google Ironwood TPUs for Claude inference, AWS claims Trainium 3 is 50% cheaper, and NVIDIA's own 10-K flags customers building workload-specific ASICs — but NVIDIA hyperscale revenue still grew 115% YoY and datacenter GPU shipments rose 19%, so merchant-GPU growth is not visibly decelerating, capping this at reaffirmed.
sensitivity: Relative $/token cost of ASICs vs GPUs and hyperscaler tolerance of software-ecosystem switching costs.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 B200/H100 median $/GPU-hour declines ≥15% at ≥2 tracked providers within one quarter, or H100 median falls below $2.50/GPU-hr for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Packaging/HBM-constrained rollout keeps rental capacity behind demand, sustaining high utilization and firm clearing prices.
rationale: D6 rental levels are essentially flat versus the prior cycle — B200 holds at ~$5.89-6.99/GPU-hr (aimultiple $6.11, RunPod $5.89, Lambda $6.69-6.99, CoreWeave $68.80/8-GPU node all unchanged) and H100 at ~$2.99-4.29 — showing prices holding up as new supply arrives, with no ≥15% decline.
sensitivity: Pace of new datacenter capacity online versus inference demand, and older-gen (H100/A100) erosion as B200/GB200 ramp.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200/advanced-compute China case-by-case licensing and the 25% Section-232 tariff, AND NVIDIA books >$2B China Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Export licensing and tariffs foreclose China compute demand, but overwhelming supply-limited non-China demand absorbs capacity, so controls trim addressable market without constraining realized revenue.
rationale: Net policy direction is loosening — the AI Diffusion Rule was rescinded and H200/MI325X moved to case-by-case licensing — while the residual constraint is immaterial to realizable revenue: NVIDIA guided $91B and grew 92% excluding China, so supply, not export policy, is the binding limit even though China compute stays foreclosed with a 25% tariff.
sensitivity: US-China policy direction and whether non-China demand stays supply-limited enough to mask any China loss.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q discloses customer financing/notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed AI-cloud/neocloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: If NVIDIA underwrote purchases via credit or revenue-share, reported demand would partly reflect NVIDIA-funded orders, creating a loop exposed to customer default — but observed demand is customer-capex-funded.
rationale: This cycle's findings show demand backed by hyperscaler self-funded capex ($660-690B), record cash revenues, and rising RPO ($27B to $30B) with no disclosed NVIDIA financing receivables; the only adjacent risk is rising customer concentration, so the vendor-financing circularity mechanism finds no primary support.
sensitivity: Disclosure of financing receivables and the financial health of concentrated neocloud/hyperscaler customers.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue (from 54%) for 2 consecutive quarters per 10-Q customerConcentration disclosure.
detail.mechanism: With over half of revenue from three buyers, any one pausing capex or in-housing to ASICs materially dents NVIDIA revenue independent of aggregate AI demand.
detail.sensitivity: Hyperscaler capex cyclicality and in-housing/ASIC decisions by the top-3 accounts.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's data-center revenue is increasingly concentrated in a few direct customers, raising demand-durability risk if any single hyperscaler slows orders or shifts to in-house silicon.
detail.title: Customer concentration risk

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or falls below 15% of Data Center revenue.
detail.mechanism: Selling rack-scale fabric (GB200 NVL72, DGX Rubin) locks customers into NVIDIA's system architecture, raising the cost and complexity of substituting merchant or ASIC compute using non-NVIDIA interconnect.
detail.sensitivity: Adoption of open interconnect standards (UALink/Ultra Ethernet) and ASIC clusters built on non-NVIDIA fabric.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's rack-scale networking is scaling faster than its compute, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus merchant accelerators and custom ASICs.
detail.title: Networking attach as systems moat

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA rpoBacklog and supply commitments grow under 10% while Data Center revenue keeps rising, or leadTimes fall below 26 weeks, for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: NVIDIA's data-center sales jumped and its next-quarter guidance rose while package and memory lead times stretched past a year, so buyers keep ordering more than the company can ship.
rationale: NVIDIA posted record data-center revenue of $75.2B, up 92% year over year and 21% sequentially, guided the next quarter up to $91.0B, supply-chain players report an order boom running into 2H26, and Anthropic's models went into production on GB300 in Azure, all while packaging lead times stretched past a year.
sensitivity: One soft quarter of data-center revenue, or lead times collapsing toward 26 weeks, would move this toward reaffirmed or weakened.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS-S/L leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM3E returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
mechanism: Memory shortages stretched NVIDIA's advanced-package lead times past a year, so packaging and memory, not wafers or orders, decide how much GPU revenue can ship.
rationale: High-bandwidth memory shortages pushed H200 and GB200 NVL72 lead times out to 36-52 weeks even as wafer starts and orders stayed plentiful, confirming that packaging and memory set the ceiling on shippable revenue, though simultaneous next-generation ramps signal future relief.
sensitivity: If the simultaneous next-generation ramps cut lead times below 26 weeks, this weakens quickly.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue growth falls below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler MI-series deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: AMD's data-center revenue grew 57% year over year with named OpenAI and Oracle deployments and a rising next-generation roadmap, so it is winning real accelerator volume rather than only sampling.
rationale: AMD grew data-center revenue to $5.8B, up 57% year over year, guided Q2 to about $11.2B, named OpenAI as a core MI450 compute partner alongside Oracle MI355X and Dell/HPE/Supermicro MI350, and held roughly 5-7% accelerator share, so the Instinct roadmap is turning into real volume rather than samples.
sensitivity: A stall in Instinct share below 6% or loss of the OpenAI ramp would weaken this.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue (D2) growth stays above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Custom accelerators stayed inside their owners' clouds with no rental market while NVIDIA's data-center revenue reaccelerated and GPUs held about 60% of one large cloud's build, so in-house chips add capacity rather than displace GPU demand.
rationale: Hyperscaler custom chips kept expanding but stayed captive with no rental market, GPUs still made up about 60% of one large cloud's AI build-out, and NVIDIA's data-center revenue reaccelerated to record levels, so in-house silicon is adding capacity rather than taking merchant-GPU growth.
sensitivity: External rental of a custom ASIC at scale, or NVIDIA growth stalling, would re-strengthen it.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 B200/H100 median dollar-per-GPU-hour declines at least 15% at two or more tracked providers within one quarter, or H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Rental rates for H100 and B200 stayed well above floor levels and NVIDIA held a 75% gross margin, so buyers still pay premium prices even as new capacity arrives.
rationale: RunPod listed H100 at $3.29/hr, above the $2.50 floor, Lambda listed flagship B200 at $6.69/hr, and NVIDIA held a 75% non-GAAP gross margin with flat guidance, so rental and vendor prices held up as new capacity arrived.
sensitivity: A sharp rental-price cut across providers as 2H26 supply lands would weaken this.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200/advanced-compute China case-by-case licensing and the 25% Section-232 tariff, and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Washington shifted H200-class China sales from denial to case-by-case review, so the policy now constrains a smaller share of demand than it did under a full ban.
rationale: Effective January 15 2026 the U.S. moved H200-class sales to China and Macau from outright denial to case-by-case review, loosening the policy so it blocks less of the addressable market than before.
sensitivity: Full license removal plus booked China revenue would break it, while a re-tightening of controls would re-strengthen it.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed AI-cloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: NVIDIA launched a revenue-sharing and credit-support program that helps AI clouds buy its systems, so part of reported demand now depends on financing NVIDIA itself provides.
rationale: NVIDIA introduced a revenue-sharing and credit-support program that helps AI clouds buy its infrastructure, which is the exact financing channel the thesis flags, so more reported demand now rests on credit NVIDIA extends.
sensitivity: Disclosed notes-receivable growth or a financed-customer default would strengthen it further, while retirement of the program would weaken it.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per 10-K/10-Q customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: NVIDIA's largest direct customer grew to 22% of revenue and its second to 14%, up from about 11% each a year earlier, so a growing share of sales rides on a few buyers.
rationale: NVIDIA's largest direct customer rose to 22% of revenue and its second to 14%, up from roughly 11% each a year earlier, with the four largest topping half of revenue, confirming that sales lean on a handful of buyers and the concentration is growing.
sensitivity: Broadening of the customer base below a 40% top-three share would weaken this.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching by selling Spectrum-X and Quantum together with Blackwell and GB300, so buyers who adopt its racks find it costlier to mix in rival accelerators.
rationale: ADJUSTED: NVIDIA now leads the data-center Ethernet switch market and sells that networking bundled with its GPUs, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus standalone accelerators and custom chips. NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching in Q1 2026 by pairing Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, and its NVLink systems cut cost per token, but no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper claim that networking scales faster than compute is not supported.
sensitivity: Networking revenue growing slower than compute, or losing the switch-market lead, would weaken it.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue (D2) growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: Each NVIDIA generation sharply lowers the cost of generating a token, so workloads that were too expensive become affordable and total GPU consumption rises.
detail.sensitivity: A generation that fails to lower cost per token, or inference demand rolling over, would undercut it.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's each-generation drop in cost per token pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs and widens the market it can serve.
detail.title: Cheaper inference pulls in demand

falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
mechanism: A rising order backlog and scarce high-bandwidth memory mean NVIDIA still cannot ship enough GPUs to fill the demand buyers are placing.
rationale: Fresh evidence deepens the gap between orders and shippable supply: server-chain players report an AI order boom spilling into 2H26 (www-digitimes-com-f88ca4e6-1), HBM is sold out through 2027 with books into 2028 and is directly gating GPU availability (www-investing-com-09606ac9-2026-07-1, finance-yahoo-com-ea29a8bf-2026-07-1), Anthropic put production inference on GB300 in Azure (nvidianews-nvidia-com-2de12e3b-1), and NVIDIA's gross margin rose to 74.9% (nvidianews-nvidia-com-7b7a02ff-2026-07-2). Simultaneous next-gen ramps expanding supply into 2H26 (www-digitimes-com-f88ca4e6-2) are a partial offset but do not relieve the memory bottleneck.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how fast high-bandwidth memory and advanced-packaging capacity catch up to order volume.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
mechanism: High-bandwidth memory and packaging capacity, not silicon wafers, set the ceiling on how many GPUs NVIDIA and AMD can build and sell.
rationale: Micron says HBM3E/HBM4 is effectively sold out through 2027 with order books into 2028 (www-investing-com-09606ac9-2026-07-1) and that shipment growth is capped by supply, not demand, directly gating NVIDIA and AMD GPU availability (finance-yahoo-com-ea29a8bf-2026-07-1); 16 non-cancelable five-year take-or-pay contracts backed by over $22B confirm the memory ceiling is binding (finance-yahoo-com-ea29a8bf-2026-07-2). Multiple accelerators reaching mass production at once (www-digitimes-com-f88ca4e6-2) does not add memory, so wafers and demand are not the limiting factor.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory-maker capacity additions from Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: Named hyperscaler wins and 57% data-center revenue growth show AMD is taking a growing slice of accelerator spending.
rationale: AMD Data Center revenue grew 57% YoY to $5.8B (www-stocktitan-net-fe36e810-2026-07-1), OpenAI named AMD a core strategic compute partner from MI450 with Oracle deploying MI355X rack-scale and Dell/HPE/Supermicro integrating MI350 (www-amd-com-49475e6c-2026-07-1), and AMD reports leading-customer forecasts exceeding expectations (ir-amd-com-f416295d-2026-07-4), with MI450 sampling now and production in H2 2026 (www-spheron-network-e5a0032c-2026-07-2) and Q2 revenue guided to ~$11.2B (www-stocktitan-net-fe36e810-2026-07-3); share is still only ~5-7% versus NVIDIA ~80% (www-amd-com-49475e6c-2026-07-2), and one analyst note is explicitly unconfirmed (www-investing-com-f24cdca0-2, www-investing-com-f24cdca0-1), but growth well above 20% with fresh named hyperscaler wins clears the break bar.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether MI450 and Helios ramp on schedule in H2 2026 and to ROCm software maturity.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Hyperscalers keep buying more merchant GPUs even as their own Trainium and TPU chips reach mass production, so in-house silicon is not eating GPU demand growth.
rationale: AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU are moving into mass production alongside the merchant parts (www-digitimes-com-f88ca4e6-2), yet merchant demand keeps booming into 2H26 (www-digitimes-com-f88ca4e6-1) and AMD's own merchant sales grew 57% (www-stocktitan-net-fe36e810-2026-07-1) while GPUs still hold ~80%+ of the accelerator market (www-amd-com-49475e6c-2026-07-2); in-house silicon is expanding capacity but is not visibly eating merchant-GPU demand growth, so the displacement claim stays weak.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to the share of new hyperscaler capacity that goes to in-house accelerators rather than merchant GPUs.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
mechanism: Scarce supply keeps hourly rental rates for H100 and B200 well above prior floors even as new capacity comes online.
rationale: Rental rates hold well above the break floor: RunPod lists H100 SXM at $3.29/hr and up to ~$3.99 across RunPod and Lambda, with H200 $4.39, B200 $5.89, B300 $7.39 (www-runpod-io-bdb62dfd-2026-07-1), and Lambda lists B200 SXM6 at $6.69/hr (lambda-ai-845323fc-1), all far above the $2.50 H100 trigger; the only lower figure is a stale Feb 2025 used-card eBay print (www-ebay-com-fe5361e5-1) that carries little weight.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to the pace at which new Blackwell and next-gen supply floods the rental market.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: U.S. licensing rules and a 25% import tariff drove NVIDIA's China data-center compute sales from billions to zero, removing a large block of addressable demand.
rationale: Reversing the prior weakened read on primary filings: NVIDIA recorded zero China Data Center Hopper shipments in Q1 FY2027 versus $4.6B a year earlier, with H200 licenses from Feb 2026 producing no revenue and carrying a 25% tariff (s201-q4cdn-com-f9f138ac-2026-07-2), and AMD took ~$440M of China-related charges with MI308 restricted and MI325 needing licenses plus a 25% tariff (ir-amd-com-c9dc9d6e-2026-07-1); the Jan 2026 shift to case-by-case review (www-bis-gov-dbeede91-2026-07-1) is a paper loosening that has not converted to revenue, so realized demand is materially constrained. This reversal rests on primary NVIDIA and AMD disclosures and spans three distinct publishers, so it is applied.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether case-by-case licenses convert into shipped, tariff-cleared China revenue.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: NVIDIA borrowed 25 billion dollars and gives startups GPUs in exchange for future revenue shares, so part of reported demand rests on financing NVIDIA itself provides.
rationale: Two concrete programs now underwrite demand: NVIDIA launched a revenue-sharing and credit-support model letting AI clouds procure its infrastructure (blogs-nvidia-com-817f7ea6-1), and it raised $25B of bonds on Jun 15 2026 to fund giving startups compute for future-revenue share, with two partners (Sharon AI, Firmus) already committed to over 200,000 GPUs (www-cnbc-com-d1364ee3-2026-07-1). NVIDIA's own announcement is primary evidence, so this upgrade from weakened is applied even though corroboration spans only two publishers.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether financed partners like Sharon AI and Firmus convert their commitments into paid cash revenue.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: A handful of large buyers, including financed partners running over 200,000 GPUs each, still account for most of NVIDIA's data-center sales.
rationale: Concentration at the top persists, with two financed partners committed to over 200,000 GPUs each under the $25B program (www-cnbc-com-d1364ee3-2026-07-1) and the same pattern acknowledged industry-wide by AMD (ir-amd-com-c9dc9d6e-2026-07-2); NVIDIA's move to open compute access to startups, model builders, enterprises, and regional players (blogs-nvidia-com-817f7ea6-1) could broaden the base, but no disclosed top-3 figure yet crosses the 40% break line, so the risk holds without clear strengthening or breaking.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether NVIDIA's push into many smaller neocloud and startup buyers actually broadens its revenue base.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: NVIDIA sells Spectrum-X and Quantum switches together with Blackwell and GB300 racks, so buyers who want the full system take NVIDIA networking too.
rationale: NVIDIA became the revenue leader in the global data-center Ethernet switch market for the first time in Q1 2026 by bundling Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300 (www-digitimes-com-8764779e-1), confirming the leadership and bundling claim; still no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper scaling claim remains unsupported and the thesis holds as previously adjusted.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether hyperscalers adopt open Ultra Ethernet fabrics instead of NVIDIA's bundled networking.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Anthropic and others put production inference on NVIDIA's newest GB300 GPUs because each generation lowers the cost of serving a token.
rationale: Anthropic moved production Claude inference onto NVIDIA's newest GB300 in Azure (nvidianews-nvidia-com-2de12e3b-1), consistent with cheaper per-token serving pulling inference onto current-generation GPUs; the direct cost-per-token metric stays hard to verify because a reliable perf-per-watt for B200 cannot be computed from conflicting TDP and FLOPS figures (www-runpod-io-59ae0a2f-2026-07-1), so support is real but modest.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether cheaper per-token inference on rival TPU and Trainium chips pulls workloads away.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: Buyers have prepaid about 18 billion dollars in deposits under non-cancelable five-year memory contracts, so much of future accelerator demand is contractually committed rather than merely forecast.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether AI-cloud buyers can fund and absorb the prepaid memory volumes they have committed to.
detail.statement: Non-cancelable multi-year HBM contracts backed by billions in customer deposits lock a large share of future accelerator demand into place and reduce the chance that today's buildout is speculative.
detail.title: Prepaid HBM take-or-pay locks in accelerator demand

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
detail.mechanism: Case-by-case licenses let some China orders through, yet the 25% tariff and the cap limiting China shipments to under half of matching U.S. sales hold realized China revenue far below the prior peak.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether case-by-case licenses convert into shipped, tariff-cleared China revenue rather than staying on paper.
detail.statement: U.S. regulators now allow case-by-case China sales of H200 and MI325X-class chips, but a 25% tariff, security conditions, and a volume cap keep any China revenue recovery small.
detail.title: Conditional China re-opening with a capped ceiling

falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
mechanism: Advanced packaging and memory output cap how many GPUs Nvidia can ship while orders and rent-back financing keep buyers lined up.
rationale: Vendor-financing now backs roughly 210,000 high-end GPUs (startupfortune) and TSMC capacity across CoWoS and HBM keeps tightening (digitimes), while Nvidia diverts wafer capacity to data-center parts over consumer chips (tomshardware df64dd8f), so demand still runs ahead of what Nvidia can serve; the increasingly financed nature of that demand keeps this a reaffirmation rather than a further strengthening.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to packaging and memory capacity additions outpacing new order growth.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
mechanism: Foundry packaging and stacked-memory output, not silicon wafers or order books, set the ceiling on how many accelerators can be built and sold.
rationale: DigiTimes reports AI demand tightening TSMC capacity specifically at CoWoS packaging and HBM and handing foundries pricing power (digitimes), and Nvidia is rationing scarce advanced capacity toward data-center GPUs (tomshardware df64dd8f), directly confirming that packaging and memory, not wafers or demand, set the realizable revenue ceiling.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to CoWoS and HBM capacity expansions catching up to demand.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: AMD needs both software compatibility and named hyperscaler wins to turn its roadmap into share, and the collapse of an open-source path for running Nvidia software on its chips removes one of those levers.
note: amd-credible-second-source: deferred: secondary-only reversal (1 distinct publishers < 3)
rationale: The most prominent open-source path for running Nvidia's software on non-Nvidia GPUs has lost funding and is winding down (vosen), removing an easy migration bridge for AMD, and no finding this cycle shows a new AMD hyperscaler win or share gain, so the case that AMD converts its roadmap into material share softens.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to named hyperscaler MI deployments and software portability improving.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Hyperscalers and Chinese firms build their own or domestic accelerators for some workloads, but this adds to total AI silicon demand rather than cutting Nvidia GPU orders.
rationale: DigiTimes shows AI demand broadening beyond GPUs into ASICs (digitimes) and Meituan trained a frontier model on roughly 50,000 domestic non-Nvidia accelerators (tech-yahoo), but both add to total AI silicon demand rather than visibly displacing GPU growth, so this stays a weak, low-conviction thesis without a clear step up.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether custom silicon starts absorbing workloads that would otherwise grow GPU orders.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
mechanism: Tight supply keeps rental prices for Nvidia GPUs elevated even as new capacity comes online.
rationale: Listed on-demand H100 rates hold at $3.99/GPU-hr at Lambda and $6.155/GPU-hr at CoreWeave (lambda, coreweave), well above the $2.50 floor, and Nvidia's supply rationing keeps prices elevated (tomshardware df64dd8f), so rental price levels are holding up despite added capacity.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a rush of new rental capacity arriving faster than demand.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: US licensing rules and criminal enforcement against chip diversion wall off China as an addressable market for Nvidia accelerators.
rationale: A high-confidence SDNY indictment over Nvidia-chip export violations (sec-gov), a Taiwan criminal raid on Supermicro tied to roughly $2.5 billion of diverted servers (tomshardware 0169805b), and an expanded Keelung smuggling probe (finance-yahoo) show intensifying enforcement that further walls off China demand, strengthening the exposure thesis.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to US policy either tightening enforcement further or reopening licensed China sales.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: Nvidia guarantees to rent back idle capacity and takes revenue share to help small clouds buy its GPUs, so part of reported demand rests on Nvidia's own credit.
rationale: A CFO-approved Nvidia program that guarantees to rent back idle capacity and takes revenue share now underwrites purchases of roughly 210,000 GPUs across named clouds Firmus and Sharon AI (startupfortune), directly confirming that reported demand increasingly leans on Nvidia-extended credit.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how much of buildout demand depends on Nvidia-extended credit versus customer cash.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: A handful of direct buyers drive most datacenter revenue, so any one cutting orders or switching to in-house silicon would dent demand.
rationale: The only customer-structure signal this cycle is Nvidia financing smaller neoclouds like Firmus and Sharon AI (startupfortune), which slightly widens the direct-buyer base but does not materially reduce reliance on a few large customers, so concentration risk holds unchanged.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how much revenue the top few direct customers represent.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Nvidia sells its switching and interconnect bundled with its GPUs, so buyers adopt the whole system and face higher costs to switch away.
rationale: DigiTimes shows AI demand broadening into networking alongside GPUs (digitimes), consistent with Nvidia selling interconnect as part of the system, but no finding this cycle compares networking growth against compute growth, so the moat claim holds at low conviction without gaining strength.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether networking revenue keeps pace with compute revenue.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Each GPU generation lowers the cost to serve a token, so more inference workloads become economical to run on Nvidia hardware.
rationale: No finding this cycle directly tests cost per token; broadening AI demand across GPUs and adjacent silicon (digitimes) is consistent with a widening served market but does not update the specific cost-per-token mechanism, so the thesis holds unchanged at low conviction.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether each generation keeps lowering cost per token.

falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Customers prepay billions on non-cancelable multi-year memory supply, so a large share of future accelerator output is already committed.
rationale: HBM supply is tightening further as AI demand strains TSMC and memory capacity (digitimes), consistent with scarce memory being locked up under long-term commitments, but no finding reports cancellations or renegotiations of take-or-pay deals, so the lock-in thesis holds at low conviction.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to any cancellation or renegotiation of the memory supply deposits.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
mechanism: Case-by-case licensing paired with a tariff, security terms, and heavy enforcement against diversion keeps the legal China channel small.
rationale: Intensifying enforcement, including an SDNY indictment (sec-gov), a Taiwan raid tied to roughly $2.5 billion of diverted servers (tomshardware 0169805b), and an expanded smuggling probe (finance-yahoo), reinforces the capped-ceiling side of the thesis by keeping the legal China channel tightly policed and small.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to changes in the tariff, volume cap, or enforcement posture.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: A funded compatibility layer or a rival software stack reaches parity such that two or more named hyperscalers move production inference off Nvidia within 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: As open-source compatibility layers lose funding and rival chips still hit software limits, buyers stay on Nvidia to avoid rewriting their software stacks.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether a well-funded software layer restores compatibility for Nvidia workloads on rival GPUs.
detail.statement: The difficulty of running existing AI software on non-Nvidia chips keeps customers on Nvidia even when rivals cut hardware prices.
detail.title: CUDA software lock-in sustains the moat

note: vendor-financed-demand-circularity: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 6 publisher domains)
customer-concentration-risk: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 5 publisher domains)
networking-attach-as-systems-moat: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 2 publisher domains)
cheaper-inference-pulls-in-demand: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 3 publisher domains)

falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
mechanism: Buyers keep placing more accelerator orders than Nvidia and its suppliers can build, so lead times and rental prices stay high.
rationale: A primary SEC filing shows a new six-year Nvidia deal for up to 40,000 GB300 GPUs, the vendor-financing program now spans about 210,000 GPUs across neoclouds, and providers are raising rental prices amid scarcity, so buyers keep ordering more than Nvidia can ship.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory and packaging supply catching up and to how much demand is propped up by Nvidia-extended financing.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
mechanism: Memory makers move capacity to HBM and hold back DRAM, so the memory and packaging bottleneck caps how many accelerators actually ship.
rationale: Three publishers report memory makers diverting wafer capacity to HBM and restricting standard DRAM, pushing AI-server rack costs up about 2% to 3% per week and 10% to 15% above baseline, which confirms memory as the binding limit on how many accelerators can ship.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory makers reversing the HBM capacity shift and restoring DRAM and packaging output.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: Buyers seeking a non-Nvidia option are turning to custom ASICs rather than AMD, so AMD's roadmap is not translating into datacenter share.
rationale: No finding this cycle shows an AMD hyperscaler win or share gain, and the only demonstrated non-Nvidia frontier path is Chinese custom ASICs rather than AMD Instinct, so the case that AMD turns its roadmap into material share softens; this reversal rests on two publishers with no AMD-primary evidence, so it is recorded but not yet applied.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to AMD landing a named hyperscaler Instinct deployment or a reported share gain.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: A frontier-scale model was trained entirely on custom AI ASICs instead of Nvidia GPUs, showing alternative silicon can take workloads that would otherwise buy merchant GPUs.
note: custom-asic-substitution: deferred: secondary-only reversal (2 distinct publishers < 3)
rationale: Two publishers report Meituan trained a 1.6-trillion-parameter frontier model entirely on more than 50,000 domestic non-Nvidia AI ASICs across 35 trillion tokens, showing custom silicon can absorb workloads that would otherwise buy merchant GPUs.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether custom-ASIC training stays an export-driven niche or spreads to Western hyperscalers at scale.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
mechanism: Providers are raising on-demand GPU rental prices as compute stays scarce, so rates hold or climb even as more capacity comes online.
rationale: Nebius raised on-demand GPU prices about 30% on June 1 and current listings hold H100 near $3.99 and B200 near $6.69 per GPU-hour, so rental levels are climbing rather than falling as capacity grows.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a wave of new capacity or memory-cost relief that lets providers cut rental rates.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: US and Taiwanese authorities are raiding and charging firms over unlicensed GPU shipments to China, keeping a large market legally off-limits.
rationale: Authorities escalated the smuggling case with raids on Supermicro's Taiwan office and employee detentions over about $2.5 billion of servers shipped to China without a license, reinforcing that enforcement keeps a large market off-limits, though only one publisher carried it this cycle.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a policy reversal that reopens licensed China sales at volume.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: Nvidia backstops purchases, shares revenue, and extends credit so capital-constrained neoclouds can buy its GPUs, so part of its reported demand rests on financing it provides itself.
rationale: A primary SEC filing plus reporting detail Nvidia's revenue-sharing and credit-support structure and an expanding backstop program covering roughly 210,000 GPUs across neoclouds, so more reported demand rests on financing Nvidia itself provides.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether financed neoclouds convert commitments into real cash revenue or default.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: Nvidia's demand is clustering into a small set of large and vendor-financed buyers, so a slowdown or default by any one of them would cut into reported demand.
rationale: The new deals cluster demand into a small set of vendor-financed neoclouds such as Firmus and Sharon AI, so a slowdown or default by any one of them would dent reported demand, keeping the concentration risk in place.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the buyer base broadens or stays clustered in a few names.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Customers keep buying complete Grace Blackwell systems that come with Nvidia networking, so the bundle raises the cost of switching to standalone accelerators.
rationale: No finding this cycle compares networking growth against compute, but customers keep buying complete Grace Blackwell systems that include Nvidia networking, so the systems bundle holds and the view is unchanged.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to open Ethernet alternatives winning networking sockets away from Nvidia.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Each Nvidia generation lowers the cost to run a token, so more inference workloads become economical to run on its GPUs.
rationale: No finding this cycle measures cost per token, and GPU compute demand stays strong enough for providers to raise prices, so the view that cheaper per-token cost widens the market is unchanged.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to rising memory and system costs eroding per-token gains or to a stall in generational efficiency.

falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Memory makers are diverting capacity to HBM to fill committed accelerator orders, so multi-year HBM commitments keep future demand in place.
rationale: Memory makers are diverting capacity to HBM to meet committed AI-accelerator orders and no finding reports any HBM contract cancellation, so the take-or-pay lock-in view holds.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to any HBM contract cancellation or renegotiation that would show the commitments are soft.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
mechanism: Aggressive enforcement against unlicensed China shipments keeps the legal, licensed channel narrow, so any revenue recovery there stays small.
rationale: Aggressive enforcement with raids and detentions over about $2.5 billion of unlicensed shipments keeps the legal China channel narrow, consistent with a small, capped recovery rather than a broad reopening.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a loosening of licensing or tariff terms that would lift the volume ceiling.

falsifiableTrigger: A funded compatibility layer or a rival software stack reaches parity such that two or more named hyperscalers move production inference off Nvidia within 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: A frontier model was trained entirely on non-Nvidia ASICs, showing teams can run large AI workloads without Nvidia's software stack when they must.
rationale: Two publishers report a 1.6-trillion-parameter frontier model trained entirely on non-Nvidia ASICs, direct evidence that large AI work can run off Nvidia's software stack, which softens the claim that software difficulty keeps customers locked in.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether non-CUDA training stays confined to export-restricted buyers or spreads to customers who could choose Nvidia.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost per S10 falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
detail.mechanism: Memory makers are moving capacity to high-margin HBM and holding back standard DRAM, so the memory portion of each AI-server bill of materials keeps rising.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory makers restoring DRAM output or HBM supply catching up to AI demand.
detail.statement: Fast-rising memory and NVMe prices are pushing up what it costs to build each AI server, which could strain buyer budgets and squeeze system margins.
detail.title: Rising memory costs inflate AI-server economics

note: prepaid-hbm-take-or-pay-locks-in-accelerator-demand: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 3 publisher domains)
conditional-china-re-opening-with-a-capped-ceiling: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 4 publisher domains)

falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
mechanism: NVIDIA's customers keep ordering GPUs faster than the company and its packaging suppliers can build and ship them, which keeps backlog high and lead times long.
rationale: Server supply-chain players report an AI order boom spilling into the second half of 2026, NVIDIA says it holds purchase orders from almost all major customers ahead of the Vera Rubin ramp, and Anthropic's Claude now runs in production on GB300 in Azure, so demand keeps pressing against supply even as capacity expands.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how quickly packaging and memory supply catch up to the order book.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
mechanism: The number of accelerators NVIDIA can actually sell is set by how much advanced packaging and stacked memory its suppliers can produce, not by wafer starts or order volume.
rationale: Suppliers are racing to add exactly the capacity the thesis names — ASE is boosting advanced packaging, Hanmi is moving into chip packaging, and NVIDIA has raised committed supply to $145 billion — which confirms packaging and stacked memory remain the binding lever, though this cycle's additions are early relief rather than a resolved bottleneck.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how fast new packaging and memory capacity from ASE, Hanmi, and others comes online.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: AMD is turning its Instinct roadmap into signed hyperscaler deployments and fast-growing data-center revenue, which builds a real second supply option to NVIDIA.
rationale: AMD landed a wave of named commitments — OpenAI as a core strategic compute partner from MI450, Meta's plan for up to 6 gigawatts of Instinct, and Oracle's MI355X racks — while data-center revenue grew 57% year over year to $5.8 billion, a clear improvement over last cycle even though Instinct still holds only about 5-7% of the accelerator market.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the OpenAI and Meta commitments convert into shipped revenue and rising share.

note: custom-asic-substitution: pending challenge lapsed (no same-direction confirmation)

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: When hyperscalers deploy their own chips such as Trainium and TPU, they buy fewer merchant GPUs, which would slow NVIDIA's and AMD's demand growth.
rationale: Although AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU are entering mass production, merchant GPUs still hold about 80% of the accelerator market and NVIDIA's order book is booming into the second half of 2026, so in-house silicon is not visibly displacing merchant-GPU demand growth this cycle.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether Trainium and TPU volumes grow faster than hyperscaler merchant-GPU orders.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
mechanism: Cloud providers keep renting NVIDIA GPUs at high hourly rates and buyers pay above list price for B200 hardware, so added capacity has not yet forced prices down.
rationale: B200 cloud rentals still sit at roughly $3.99 to $6.69 per GPU-hour, used B200 hardware trades near $45,000 above its $40,000 list, and NVIDIA holds a 75% gross margin, so prices are holding despite expanding supply; the lone soft data point, a $799 used H100 figure, is stale from early 2025 and not reliable.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the wave of new accelerator supply in the second half of 2026 outpaces rental demand.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: U.S. export rules force AMD to license or halt China GPU shipments and add a 25% tariff, which cuts off addressable demand and triggers inventory write-downs.
rationale: Export rules cost AMD about $440 million in inventory and related charges in 2025, restrict MI308, require licenses for MI325, and force US inspection plus a 25% tariff on any China shipment, giving the risk a concrete price tag it lacked before.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to U.S. policy changes on China licensing and tariffs.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: NVIDIA shares revenue and extends credit so AI clouds can buy its chips, which means part of reported demand rests on financing NVIDIA itself provides rather than customer cash.
rationale: NVIDIA has launched a revenue-sharing and credit-support model that lets AI clouds procure its infrastructure, which is the exact mechanism the thesis warns about and shows vendor-extended credit increasingly underwriting reported demand.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how much booked demand relies on NVIDIA's own financing versus customer cash.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: A small set of large cloud buyers accounts for most accelerator revenue, so if one slows orders or shifts to in-house chips, demand drops sharply.
rationale: AMD's disclosure that a few customers drive most of its business shows accelerator revenue concentration is an industry-wide pattern, and NVIDIA still leans on a handful of large buyers; its new push to reach startups and regional players could broaden the base over time but has not yet changed the concentration picture.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether NVIDIA's reach into smaller and regional buyers actually widens its revenue base.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: NVIDIA sells its Spectrum-X and Quantum networking together with Blackwell GPUs, so customers who buy the compute also take the network, which raises the cost of switching to rival chips.
rationale: NVIDIA became the revenue leader in data-center Ethernet switching for the first time in Q1 2026 by bundling Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, confirming the systems-moat claim; as before, no finding compares networking growth to compute growth, so the sharper version stays unsupported.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether customers keep buying NVIDIA networking with the GPUs rather than mixing in standalone switches.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Each new NVIDIA generation does more work per chip and per watt, which lowers the cost of running each inference request and makes more workloads worth putting on its GPUs.
rationale: Blackwell Ultra (B300) delivers about 50% more compute and memory per chip than B200, continuing the generational efficiency gains that lower the cost of each inference request, and Anthropic now runs Claude in production on GB300 in Azure, an inference workload landing on NVIDIA hardware.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether each generation keeps cutting cost per token faster than rivals.

falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Multi-year, non-cancelable memory contracts and large upfront deposits commit buyers to pay for accelerator supply years ahead, so much of the planned buildout is contracted rather than speculative.
rationale: NVIDIA raised committed supply, including inventory purchase commitments and prepaids, to $145 billion and holds purchase orders from almost all major customers, showing multi-year commitments across the memory and accelerator chain are still growing rather than being cancelled.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to any cancellation or renegotiation of the memory take-or-pay commitments.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
mechanism: Regulators let AMD and NVIDIA ship some China chips only under licenses, inspections, and a 25% tariff, which caps how much revenue the China channel can recover.
rationale: The AMD disclosure matches the thesis exactly: China shipments of MI325-class chips are allowed only under licenses and US inspection and carry a 25% tariff, with MI308 restricted, keeping any China revenue recovery small and conditional.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether Washington keeps the tariff, inspections, and volume cap in place.

falsifiableTrigger: A funded compatibility layer or a rival software stack reaches parity such that two or more named hyperscalers move production inference off Nvidia within 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Rewriting AI software to run on non-NVIDIA chips is hard, so customers stay on NVIDIA even when AMD sells cheaper hardware.
rationale: OpenAI named AMD a core strategic compute partner from MI450, Meta committed to up to 6 gigawatts of Instinct, and MI350X is generally available on neoclouds running ROCm 7.0, showing major buyers adopting non-NVIDIA hardware despite the software switching cost; production inference has not yet clearly moved off NVIDIA, so the moat is eroding rather than broken.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how mature AMD's ROCm software becomes for large AI workloads.

falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost per S10 falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
mechanism: When DRAM and flash prices climb, each AI server costs more to build, which eats into system margins and can push buyers to trim orders.
rationale: No finding this cycle confirms fast-rising memory or NVMe prices, while ASE and Hanmi are adding packaging and HBM tooling capacity, which points toward easing memory supply rather than a price squeeze; the only mild support is that B300 carries about 50% more memory per chip, raising per-server memory content.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to the direction of DRAM and HBM prices as new memory and packaging capacity comes online.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Combined accelerator output across NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google fails to rise for 2 consecutive quarters, or packaging capacity per S10 stops expanding.
detail.mechanism: When several vendors bring next-generation chips to volume at the same time and packaging capacity grows, the total number of accelerators buyers can get rises.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether packaging and memory capacity keep pace with the multi-vendor ramp.
detail.statement: A simultaneous mass-production ramp of NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google accelerators is set to expand total accelerator supply in the second half of 2026.
detail.title: Multi-vendor accelerator supply wave in second-half 2026

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Any one of the named AMD mega-deals with Meta, OpenAI, or Oracle slips its deployment schedule by more than 1 quarter, or AMD Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: AMD's accelerator revenue rests on a few giant deployments, so if one partner delays or cancels, its data-center growth stalls.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the timing and follow-through of the Meta and OpenAI commitments.
detail.statement: AMD's data-center accelerator growth depends on a small set of very large commitments, which concentrates both its upside and its downside in a handful of partners.
detail.title: AMD growth leans on a few mega-deals

note: cuda-software-lock-in-sustains-the-moat: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 6 publisher domains)
